title: Natural Disaster Recovery Program Act of 2025
short_title: Natural Disaster Recovery Program Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.
plain_summary: This bill creates a new disaster recovery program to help states and tribal governments pay for things they still need after major disasters happen. It makes it easier for areas to get declared as disaster zones by having FEMA look more closely at local impacts and recent disaster history. The bill also improves help for people whose homes are damaged by letting FEMA do repairs directly when contractors are hard to find, giving extra help to people with disabilities, and letting people stay in temporary housing for up to 24 months instead of 18 months. FEMA will also be able to build new permanent homes when it costs less than other options.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Natural Disaster Recovery Program Act of 2025. The law has ten main sections that cover different parts of disaster recovery including setting up a special fund for disasters, helping people with unmet needs after disasters, making it easier to get disaster declarations, helping people repair and rebuild homes, creating emergency home repair programs, improving the appeals process for disaster assistance, requiring reports to Congress about disaster declarations, having oversight reviews, and studying how long it takes to close disaster cases.

sections.title: Section 2  Natural Disaster Recovery Program
sections.text: This section creates a new disaster recovery program. A special fund called the Natural Disaster Recovery Reserve Fund will be set up at the Treasury Department. This fund will hold money that Congress approves for specific major disasters. The money can be used to help states and Indian tribal governments with unmet needs after major disasters and to provide training and support to help them manage disaster assistance better. If disaster recovery groups do not use all their money within six years, any leftover money goes back into the fund unless the President gives them up to four more years to use it. The President must study what needs are not being met within ninety days after a disaster is declared. This study must look at all damage from the disaster, how federal assistance has been spent, effects on schools and transportation and housing and healthcare and people who had to move, and what needs are still not met. The President must make this information available to the public but must protect personal information about people who get help. States and tribal governments can use up to thirteen percent of their grant money for administrative costs like audits and oversight. The President can also provide technical help and training to make sure grant recipients follow federal laws. Money from the fund will be given out based on how much unmet need each area has. Each state or tribal government will decide how to spend their money and will not need to submit spending plans beforehand. Grant recipients will get half their money right away. To get the other half, they must report to Congress and an inspector general about how they spent the first half and get certification from an auditor that no money was wasted. States and tribal governments must follow proper purchasing rules and require the same from groups they give money to. This money must add to other disaster assistance, not replace it. Federal agencies can use environmental reviews that have already been done by other federal agencies instead of doing new ones. The President must identify best ways to handle disaster recovery and share these practices with grant recipients within one year.

sections.title: Section 3  Unmet Needs Assistance
sections.text: This section creates a new program for unmet needs assistance. Governors and heads of Indian tribal governments can ask the President for grants to help with unmet needs after major disasters. The President can set aside ten percent of the estimated total money that will be given for disaster grants to fund this unmet needs program. The President must figure out this amount within one hundred eighty days after each major disaster and this amount will not be reduced even if the estimates change. This set aside money will not reduce other disaster assistance that is already available. States and tribal governments can use unmet needs grants for disaster related home repair and rebuilding help for families, help for families who cannot get enough assistance from other sources, other disaster services that reduce suffering and help disaster victims, and economic and business recovery activities including planning and technical help for long term economic recovery. Every six months after getting funds, states and tribal governments must report to the federal emergency management administrator about how they decide to spend the money, how they divide up the money, and their process for public notice and comment. They can spend up to five percent of the money on administrative costs for helping individuals and households.

sections.title: Section 4  Further Considerations for Disaster Declarations
sections.text: This section changes how disaster declarations are made. When the Federal Emergency Management Agency administrator makes recommendations to the President about declaring major disasters or emergencies, the administrator must give more weight to severe local impact from the disaster and whether multiple other disasters have happened in the same area in the past five years. The administrator must change agency policies and guidance and regulations to follow these new requirements. Within one year after this law is passed, the administrator must send a report to Congress about how this section has been implemented. The report must describe changes made to guidance and policies and regulations and the number of major disasters and emergencies that have been declared based on these new criteria.

sections.title: Section 5  Repair and Rebuilding
sections.text: This section changes the rules for repair and rebuilding assistance. The President can provide money to help repair homes that people own and live in, utilities, and residential infrastructure like private access roads that were damaged by a major disaster or made inaccessible for people with disabilities. The President can also provide direct assistance to people who cannot use financial assistance and when there are not enough resources available. This direct help can be for repairing homes and utilities and infrastructure and for hazard mitigation measures that reduce the chance of future damage. People who get this direct assistance usually cannot get other types of housing assistance unless the administrator decides otherwise. This assistance can be used together with other sources of money for repairing and rebuilding homes. People who get this assistance do not have to show that they cannot get help from other sources except insurance money. The President can also provide help with buying or building homes if the President thinks it costs less than other housing solutions. The rules for who can get help are expanded so that any home damaged by a major disaster can get assistance, not just homes that are completely uninhabitable.

sections.title: Section 6  FEMA Emergency Home Repair Program
sections.text: This section creates an emergency home repair program. The Federal Emergency Management Agency can now provide minor repairs up to habitability for homes that people own and live in that were damaged by disasters so survivors can safely shelter in place, if Congress provides the money. Within fifteen days after a major disaster is declared, the federal coordinating officer must tell the state governor or state coordinating officer about all sheltering and housing options that are available. Minor repairs up to habitability means the minimum standards for permanent housing described in federal regulations. The Federal Emergency Management Agency administrator must issue final regulations to implement these changes within two years after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 7  Appeals of Individuals and Households Program Benefits
sections.text: This section improves the appeals process for disaster assistance. The time period for getting temporary housing assistance is extended from eighteen months to twenty four months. The Federal Emergency Management Agency must change its regulations so that any applicant who appeals a decision about their eligibility for assistance gets any documentation that was used to make the decision including inspection documents, a description of the reasons for the decision and recommended steps to fix a denial including a list of additional documentation the applicant can provide, and any inspection documents within ten days after an inspection is completed.

sections.title: Section 8  Report to Congress on Major Disaster Declarations
sections.text: This section requires a report to Congress. Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Federal Emergency Management Agency administrator must send a report to Congress with information about each major disaster declared in the five years before this law was passed. The report must include the process used to determine individual household need, the most common reasons for denying individual and household assistance, the rate of appeals of denials, how long the agency takes to make decisions about eligibility for individual assistance for each disaster, information on the use of Disaster Legal Services and the number of applicants who were eligible, the total number of Disaster Legal Services volunteers, the number of volunteers activated for each disaster, how long it took to set up disaster recovery centers after each disaster declaration, the number of disaster recovery centers opened for each disaster, the process for selecting locations for disaster recovery centers, the average time disaster recovery centers stayed open, the average time individual survivors spend at the centers, and a description of outreach efforts in low income and rural and underserved communities about available federal emergency management assistance.

sections.title: Section 9  Review by Comptroller General
sections.text: This section requires oversight by the Comptroller General. Within five years after this law is passed, the Comptroller General of the United States must conduct a review of the fiscal controls of states that receive assistance under the unmet needs program created in this law and make recommendations about the program to Congress.

sections.title: Section 10  GAO Study on Timing of Closing Out Disaster Recovery
sections.text: This section requires a study by the Government Accountability Office. Within one year after this law is passed, the Comptroller General of the United States must submit a report to Congress about how long it takes the Federal Emergency Management Agency administrator to officially close out each major disaster declared under the Stafford Act.
